speaker: interviewer
text: --1995, and it is an interview with Joshua Degani. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mr. Degani, your-- your neighbors, were they-- had-- had they-- had they been able to leave Leipzig? Had people been making-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were successful in leaving the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frankly, I was not too much focused on neighbors. But I know that quite a number left Leipzig. And this includes some of-- of my family members, like my-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my grandmother's sisters, some could escape or leave. I don't know what the background is and how it came about. But some left, for whatever was possible at that time. And I remember the names Mandelbaum, Spiegel, Stein-- not all of them, but some of them could leave. And I remember, later on, that, uh-- I know later on, because I met one cousin of my mother, Rosa Spiegel-- met in the United States. And later, we visited her. She could take residence in Belgium. And she is the one who is still in correspondence with me, 94 years old and sickly now and having problems now. And I hope she will live many other good years. But she is one who could escape with her parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that because she had the money to do so?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I am not so sure. It was partially luck and partially intuition and partially maybe money. Some of them had money, which I know. And the money apparently played some role in it. At that time, they still could. Later on, this opportunity was not given anymore. I know who could not. This was my grandmother's brother, Wolf. And he had-- I remember, they visited with my grandmother and us very often and had two wonderful cousins, Margaret and Sophie. And I liked them as a child. They were older than I was, and they came to play. They did never make it. And coming back a little bit, tracking back, I forgot to mention that my father-- my father's real profession was an engineer. He was an engineer but could not practice it in Germany, even before Hitler. And surely not after Hitler, but even could not practice his profession as-- as trader. And it was very, very difficult because, at that time already, 'Don't buy from Jews' and 'Boycott the Jews.' And all this was a slogan over Germany. And this was an enormous, enormous hardship on all of us. And now, coming back to the point where I was sent by the Leipzig-- the Israelitische Religionsgemeinde, to Steckelsdorf, near Berlin, which is a Hachshara camp. At-- at that time, I did not know all the possible ramifications, and I did not know what further developed in Leipzig, where my brother remained and my sisters remained at that time, and my grandmother. And everybody were-- was still alive, which was in the year 1939-- something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you chosen to be in this group?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was again a matter of luck-- luck. There were two-- two people from our city, Oskar Deutsche and I. We were the only ones who were sent from the Israelitische Religionsgemeinde zu Leipzig. Apparently, from each city they got a number of spaces to-- or a number of people they could send there. Over there, I believe we were over 100 children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you don't know how you were chosen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't know how I was chosen, but I was lucky, and I am forever grateful to the Israelitische Religionsgemeinde, whom I still correspond with. And every New Year's, I send them a-- a Happy New Year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you explain what that organization was about?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the Religion-- this was the congregation of the-- of the Jews. It was established, if my memory does not mislead me, by Carlebach, or in the time of Carlebach, who was a great teacher and rabbi over there-- a great man. And he died before the Holocaust. I believe it was the year 1936, 1937. But, again, I might be off by the years, because I cannot be too exact. And he established. And they were a kind of a helpful organization to people who were in need, and especially to children. And they showed their proficiency when they did what they did. Because this was the first time. They helped me the second time. They-- when I came back to Leipzig for one night from Hachshara, they helped me again, with this other small group, to escape from Leipzig. But coming back to Leipzig, to-- to my young children, to my children's year, there were things which are-- were very impressive on me. So I could not really reconcile that such cultural place, with such cultural background-- and there was a cultural background-- Goethe, Schiller, and the monuments and all this-- could really reach the deepest, deepest, agonizing place in history of Jews when they did what they did to Jews. They express the hatred. We could not go anymore. And these rules came about, and I read about, and I knew about when my mother wrote me that they cannot go anymore to the public park, which was the-- closed. Forbidden to Jews. They could not go anymore to the movies. We could not go-- we could not go anymore to any public event! And even-- And when the war broke out, in 1939, we could not go to the shelters anymore. And I witnessed it, because I came back, and this one day when I came back, a bombardment occurred, and we could not go to the shelter. Jews had to stay in their apartments. And we are at risk, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children were with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I escaped-- now I'm coming to the part without finishing the other part. Because what happens-- in this time, I was at Hachshara, that my brother and my sister and my mother were forced, under the new rules, to deliver all their [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] valuables, all their jewelry, what they had. And we had-- we had some. We had many, because we were wealthy. And the radio. We could not listen any more to radio. We were not allowed. Jews were not allowed. And how it should be delivered and when delivered. And later on, there was a story that they-- not a story, it was a fact. They came to our apartment and raided it in order to find something which was not delivered. I don't know how all these things came about. I just read, later on, when a protocol was made and when my brother related these things to a lawyer to try to tell them, this you took from us, this we want back. We did not succeed much. This was-- When we succeeded in something very symbolic. But all this was then later made like it-- it was not so. And it was in such confusing ways. They said we-- they took it because a German law demanded it that we give it to them, because it was kind of penalty put on all the Jews. Something to that effect. How exactly, I cannot formulate. Not at this time, I could never formulate it, because I did not have all the documentation. But from what I read, it was that it simply was taken. What-- when we left-- my brother and I-- when we left with this small group of 16, which I will come about soon to tell, then we had nothing. We left without anything. It was just a small knapsack on our-- on my back. That was all. All what we had was either taken away or destroyed by the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can you imagine a family of five persons-- three sisters, two brothers, two parents-- and an additional family of my grandmother and my uncle and my-- my aunts. That nothing-- nothing but nothing remained, and that we were just with our naked body-- naked body-- forced to leave. And this was, of course, even better than to be destroyed. And about the part of destruction, I will tell you now.

speaker: interviewee
text: We, my brother and I, were in a group which was formed by the Israelitische Religionsgemeinde sent to Zagreb. And this was an adventure. But we felt it more than adventure than kind of strict rescue mission. But it was apparently necessary. Before we were sent, we met-- oh! My mother, may she rest in peace, was the one who accompanied us to the Israelitische Religionsgemeinde, where we could say goodbye. And from there it was then to the Leipziger Hauptbahnhof, where my brother and I and other 12 youngsters would be going to the annexed Austria, which was then Vienna, and from Vienna to Graz. But it was a heartbreaking time, because my mother-- we saw her the last time at that point. It was the most heartbreaking time-- time at our life, to knowingly separate from her that-- with the knowledge that it is not-- most probably not going to be a soon reunification. Also, we tried to believe that there will be a reunification. But we knew that things became so bad that it might not happen. And so we said goodbye-- Auf Wiedersehen-- at that time. We kissed. And whenever I come back to that point, I shudder. It is such a terrible memory, that this had to be the last time I saw my mother. It was different with my father. When he was taken away, we did not know, but it was not so-- it was more-- it was smoother, at the time. It was cruel, but it was smoother at that time, because we lived more in the hope that this is just some temporary matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Especially when they told us over the [? Rote ?] [? Kreuz ?] that this was Schutzhaft. Schutzhaft, my father was, as if taken to protect him. You see? When they say 'to protect him,' so we felt-- and oh, we were innocent children.

speaker: interviewee
text: We felt, they are going to protect him that nothing should happen to him. So it was some kind of feeling which was not that bad. It became bad, of course, in years to come. But our group went to Graz, and there we stayed for a week--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: --till an opportunity-- we traveled by train.

speaker: interviewer
text: In secret?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- it was not in secret from the German-- from the Leipziger government or the German-- perhaps with the agreement of the German authorities, most probably because they got a lot of money. Because otherwise, how would it come about? And they said, we are children under 14 years old, and this should be something where they should benefit of the money, and we should be able to go. Left to our fate, whatever happened, we encountered all kinds of terrible things on the way there, because we had to wait. We waited on the front-- on the-- on the border between Yugoslavia and Austria-- or Germany. It was called already Germany. And, in order to cross the border, we had to live on a farm. On-- we are not eating and drinking for days. And we were cursed by the people we passed-- as children! I remember the curses they put on us, like, Juden schwein, and all this, which is like, pigs-- Jewish pigs.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were hidden in a farm for--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were-- we were kind of accommodated the farm before we could cross the border. And at the border, we were accompanied by an adult, apparently also a paid assistant or paid helper, who should help us to go illegally into Yugoslavia and to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any kind of papers with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had papers by the Nazi authorities, with the Nazi stamps-- who we are and what we are and when we are born. And I remember the J stamp, which shows Jew, just in case somebody doubts. So this was an-- also a requirement. Any Jewish person must have stamped in any document the Jew-- the J stamp, which showed Jew. And any man who did not have a Jewish name must have an addition of Moshe, I believe it was, and the woman had to have an addition of Sarah. But it did not apply to my mother, because she was called [NON-ENGLISH], so this was a Jewish name by itself. And also not to my father, who would not need it, because he was in Dachau, but was also called Chaim [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So whoever-- or the Jew's name was-- to be added was Chaim, I believe-- Chaim or Moshe-- I don't remember. I think it was Chaim. Were there are girls in your group as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our group, there were no girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: All boys.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here I have some kind of problem with myself, because at a later point, when my mother wrote me-- and I have letters here, or rather postcards here-- she asked-- and there was a group forming of-- of girls. If she-- when she asked if girls-- if my sister should come also, we thought it too dangerous. And it was then that they tried-- perhaps-- that my sisters, or at least the two adult sisters, and my two cousins-- Wolf, which I mentioned before-- should join.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we-- or I-- considered it dangerous, which it-- which it was. Because when we crossed the border, after we crossed the border, I heard that some did not make it in other groups which were formed in other places-- that did not make it. And this brought me to the concept that it is dangerous. But who could anticipate what it's going to be, that my sisters and my relatives are going to die in a most cruel way, which I will describe later?

speaker: interviewee
text: And there we went to Zagreb. And in Zagreb, again, the Jewish congregation received us very well. We were accommodated there and were helped there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Zagreb?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in Zagreb about six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: We studied. It was courses. We had courses in Hebrew and courses in English and in history and in religion. And they were very benevolent to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in homes?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we lived in homes and ate-- those who could not accommodate us with food, we went to other places, to outside places, to eat. And even for our entertainment was concern. I remember one lawyer, his weakness was that he wanted to see us entertained. So it was-- it was a good congregation who helped us there. And we were further help-- further benefiting of relatives which we had there. [INAUDIBLE]. We had a married cousin of my mother there. And she helped us, and we ate by her. And her parents also lived there. And so it was a relatively good time. And this kind of smoothed over what happened to my mother and sisters while they were alive in Leipzig and while the war was going on. Because we escaped, and our group was sent, in the war-- the war was already in full flare in 1939, and this was in the middle of the war, or in the beginning of the war, where our escape succeeded. And they were very worrisome about our fate and followed up what happened on the war zone. And the rescue continued, because the war was going on, and the-- they anticipated the bombardments which followed later, on Yugoslavia and the conquest of Yugoslavia. And rightly so, because we were put together again on a certain day which I don't remember. I know it was not far away from Passover. It was 10 days or 12 days or 14 days before Passover. We were put together and went from Zagreb to Belgrade, to reorganize-- I don't know the exact reason-- right to Belgrade. But from Belgrade we could escape. We were, again, called, each one by a messenger, to where we were called to come to the rail station and to go from there to a place we did not know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now-- now did you-- did you feel that you were in danger along this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. At that point, we felt already that some things were going on. But again, as youngster we did-- youngsters, we did not feel the full strength, the full force, of a war which was raging already strongly. But we became more-- the aware-- the awareness, for me, at least-- and I was the oldest. My brother is two and a half years younger-- was quite clear. That something-- that things, bad things, are happening. And at that time, don't forget, my mother was-- my mother and sisters and all were still alive. Everybody was still alive, and we did not know about the cruelties which occurred in many places already. The more cruelties. We did not even know the more cruel things which occurred in Leipzig itself, which occurred, which were witnesses by me because of the race-- race-- racial persecution. But at-- at that point, when we were gathering, only one was a miss. One could not make it to the railway station. We escaped again. And this was one day before Belgrade, where we were in the last few days, was bombarded by the Nazis. At that time-- at that time-- it was the heaviest bombardment. There were later larger bombardments, I believe, in Holland and maybe Denmark. I'm not sure. But at that time it was the largest bombardment. And then we were going by railway-- by rail, by train, to Saloniki.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long a trip was that, to Saloniki?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a few hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you identified as-- as Jews? Were you persecuted on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was nothing. I mean, nothing. Except that we entered the train, and that we were traveling, and nobody really cared except for those people who organized us and who helped us escape. And I remember only one name. It is [NON-ENGLISH]. She organized it with-- help us. I don't remember, and I don't think there was anything else. This was all very short-lived, from the call to the railway station, from the railway station in Belgrade, from Belgrade to Saloniki. This was just a ride. It was a rail-- a train ride, and we arrived in Saloniki.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you went through the borders?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- apparently no obstacles, because we went through the borders. Maybe the person who led us could identify us as, uh-- as fluchtlinger-- as those who were persecuted. And they agreed to let us out and let us in without any obs-- and-- and maybe because they saw children-- youngsters. Or maybe there were other reasons. Or maybe there was money involved. I don't know. But the fact is that this was a smooth transition from Belgrade to Saloniki. This was Greece.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there-- was there the same adult who traveled with you from Leipzig through--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was-- it was the same. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember. I don't remember who it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: A man? A Jewish man?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a man. I am not sure if the person was Jewish. I'm not sure it was Jewish. I-- even in Saloniki, I don't know-- Our stay in Saloniki was also very short. It was about one night. And again, we-- we stayed at one place altogether. And again we went to the-- or we were near the railway station. And again we went from Saloniki across the border into Turkey. And again it was a smooth transition. We were not handicapped at the border. And we could go from there, by train, to Ankara, near the Bosphorus. And there we stayed for 10 or 14 days. I don't remember well. But, again, it was all before Passover, so it was apparently-- altogether, the traveling was two or three weeks before Passover. Because this is a very clear date which is in my mind. Then from Turkey, where we stayed about a week or so-- and again we were accommodated pretty well by people over there. We were fed, and we got accommodations over there. The food was all right. The people were all right. And we, from there, could travel without any hindrance from [INAUDIBLE] to Aleppo-- Aleppo. Which is in Syria. And this was on Passover. This was where we arrived. When we arrived in Aleppo, it was Passover. Because [INAUDIBLE] Jews brought us matzohs. They know about our coming. And they spoke-- they spoke a language which we did not understand. I understood some English, because some spoke English. I think that they spoke more French over there. But they spoke, and we were there a few days-- Passover.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a Seder there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- I am not sure. I think it was. I think there was kind of ceremony said. There was kind of ceremony, but this is not any-- any more clear in my mind, because it was still a-- a-- a problematic flucht-- it was still something problematic to go. So I don't think that I dwelt, as a youngster, on the ceremony of a Seder or not. Although we had at home-- at our home, we celebrated Passover, of course. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you remember that you remember the matzohs.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I remember it was Passover. It was matzohs, and it was, um-- what you say in the prayer-- [SPEAKING HEBREW]-- that everybody can come and eat from the matzoh. And I think they practiced. The Jews of Aleppo were very wealthy, and they could easily accommodate us. And we did not stay too many days over there. Because, from there, we went to Beirut.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where your ultimate destination was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, at that time it was made already clear to us that it would be Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Palestine one of the places that you had written to, when you were back in Leipzig?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it was, but I saw it as a hopeless enterprise. Because, as I mentioned before, you needed a lot of money to go to Palestine at that time. So I rather-- at that time, when I was youngster, I went to the-- to the consul. I wrote to the French-- French consul, to Italy, at that time, to the Italian consul, which also became later the ally of Nazi Germany. Mussolini. But I wrote to Denmark, Danish, and Swedish. And all this with not much avail. No avail.
